title: Sacituzumab govitecan (Trodelvy) – ASCENT‑07 Phase 3 Trial
description: Phase 3 ASCENT‑07 clinical trial evaluating sacituzumab govitecan‑hziy (Trodelvy) in metastatic breast cancer, representing a highly anticipated oncology drug study.
oncology_drug_check: Breast cancer (HR+/HER2‑negative metastatic), drug (sacituzumab govitecan‑hziy)
development_status: As of 2025-11-07, the ASCENT-07 primary analysis was announced, indicating that the trial did not meet its primary endpoint of BICR-assessed PFS. Overall survival data is still immature, but an early trend favoring Trodelvy was observed, and patient follow-up will continue to further assess OS. The safety profile was consistent with previous Trodelvy breast cancer studies, with no new safety signals identified. The trial remains active for OS follow-up, and Gilead plans to present the full ASCENT-07 data at an upcoming medical conference.
drug_indication: The drug under study is sacituzumab govitecan (Trodelvy). While Trodelvy is approved for second-line-or-later metastatic TNBC and for certain pre-treated HR+/HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer patients, the ASCENT-07 trial specifically investigates its use as a first-line cytotoxic chemotherapy after progression on endocrine therapy in adult patients with HR+/HER2-negative (IHC 0, IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-) locally advanced/inoperable or metastatic breast cancer.
mechanism_context: Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy (Trodelvy) is a Trop-2–directed antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) that delivers SN-38 (the active metabolite of irinotecan, a topoisomerase I inhibitor) via a proprietary hydrolyzable linker. This ADC targets Trop-2 (a cell-surface antigen highly expressed in many tumor types) and produces activity both in Trop-2–expressing tumor cells and in the tumor microenvironment through a bystander effect. Trop-2 is highly expressed in >90% of breast and lung cancers, and the SN-38 payload is a topoisomerase I inhibitor.
progress_timeline_date: On 2025-11-07, Gilead announced that the ASCENT-07 study did not meet its primary endpoint of progression-free survival (PFS) at the primary analysis. The trial enrolled 654 patients across nearly 30 countries. Overall survival (OS), a key secondary endpoint, was not mature at the time of the primary analysis, but an early trend favoring Trodelvy was observed, and follow-up will continue to assess OS. The trial was initiated and enrolled between approximately 2023 and 2025, with abstracts/presentations in 2023/2024 and the primary analysis announced in late 2025. Exact registry start and primary/estimated completion dates were not provided in the press release or abstract, but can be found on ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT05840211).
trial_design: ASCENT-07 is a Phase 3, randomized, open-label, global study. Patients were randomized 2:1 to receive either sacituzumab govitecan-hziy (10 mg/kg IV on Days 1 and 8 of a 21-day cycle) or treatment of physician’s choice (TPC) single-agent chemotherapy (capecitabine, paclitaxel, or nab-paclitaxel). Treatment continued until BICR-verified progression or unacceptable toxicity. The patient population included adults (≥18 years) with histologically confirmed HR+/HER2-negative (HER2 IHC 0 or HER2-low [IHC1+, IHC2+/ISH–]), locally advanced inoperable or metastatic disease, previously treated with endocrine therapy and eligible for first-line cytotoxic chemotherapy, with measurable disease per RECIST v1.1 and ECOG 0–1. Key exclusion criteria included prior treatment targeting topoisomerase I. The study enrolled approximately 654 patients. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival (PFS) assessed by Blinded Independent Central Review (BICR) per RECIST v1.1. Key secondary endpoints included overall survival (OS), objective response rate (ORR), quality of life (QoL; EORTC QLQ‑C30 domains), safety, pharmacokinetics, and immunogenicity.
related_approved_in_trial_drugs: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for HER2‑low HR+/HER2‑negative metastatic breast cancer; standard single‑agent chemotherapies used as comparators (capecitabine, paclitaxel, nab‑paclitaxel)
biomarker_requirements: HR‑positive; HER2‑negative including HER2 IHC 0 or HER2‑low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH‑). No other specific biomarker requirements.
geographic_scope: Global across nearly 30 countries; examples include Poland, Portugal, Singapore, South Africa, South Korea
key_competing_drugs_trials: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (HER2‑low HR+/HER2‑negative metastatic breast cancer); standard single‑agent chemotherapy regimens such as capecitabine and taxanes
current_standard_of_care: After progression on endocrine therapy, sequential single‑agent chemotherapy is standard in HR+/HER2‑negative metastatic breast cancer; common agents include taxanes and capecitabine. HER2‑low patients may receive trastuzumab deruxtecan in later lines per guidelines.
primary_endpoints: Progression‑free survival (PFS) by Blinded Independent Central Review per RECIST v1.1
target_population: Adults with inoperable, locally advanced or metastatic HR‑positive, HER2‑negative breast cancer who have received prior endocrine therapy and are candidates for cytotoxic chemotherapy; documented radiographic progression; eligible for capecitabine, nab‑paclitaxel, or paclitaxel. Prior endocrine therapy must include at least two lines in the metastatic setting or recurrence within 24 months of adjuvant endocrine therapy.
trial_progress_evidence: Study started 2023‑05‑08; enrollment reached 654 patients across nearly 30 countries; primary PFS analysis on 2025‑11‑07 did not meet significance; overall survival immature but trending favorable; study continues with OS follow‑up; no new safety signals identified; primary and study completion estimated for April 2028.
line_of_therapy: First chemotherapy for locally advanced/metastatic HR+/HER2‑negative breast cancer after prior endocrine therapy
previous_phase_results_summary: Phase 3 TROPiCS‑02 trial showed a significant PFS benefit and later overall survival benefit for sacituzumab govitecan in HR+/HER2‑negative metastatic breast cancer; Phase 3 ASCENT trial demonstrated significant PFS and OS improvements in pre‑treated triple‑negative breast cancer.
secondary_endpoints: Overall survival (OS), Objective response rate (ORR) by BICR per RECIST v1.1, patient‑reported outcomes/quality of life (e.g., EORTC QLQ‑C30 physical functioning at week 16), safety
comparator_control_arm: Treatment of physician’s choice single‑agent chemotherapy: capecitabine, paclitaxel, or nab‑paclitaxel
study_design: Global, open-label, randomized, parallel assignment, Phase 3; 2:1 randomization to sacituzumab govitecan monotherapy versus treatment of physician's choice; treatment until BICR‑verified progression or unacceptable toxicity
safety_signals_modifications: No new safety signals or protocol modifications reported; safety profile consistent with prior Trodelvy breast cancer studies.
mechanism_of_action: Trop‑2‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate with a proprietary hydrolyzable linker that delivers SN‑38 (a topoisomerase I inhibitor) to Trop‑2‑expressing cells and the tumor microenvironment, providing a bystander effect.

title: Giredestrant
description: Giredestrant is an oral selective estrogen receptor degrader studied in the lidERA Phase III trial as adjuvant endocrine therapy for ER‑positive, HER2‑negative early‑stage breast cancer.
oncology_drug_check: ER‑positive, HER2‑negative early‑stage breast cancer – drug (giredestrant)
development_status: Based on Roche’s public announcement (2025-11-18) and the ClinicalTrials.gov record (last update 2025-11-21), lidERA had a positive pre-planned interim analysis and the sponsor has announced positive Phase III results. ClinicalTrials.gov indicates the trial status as Active (not recruiting) in the registry view extracted during research (registry metadata shows active status and was updated following the announcement). Overall development status for giredestrant: Active clinical development across multiple Phase III trials (lidERA adjuvant, evERA, persevERA, pionERA, heredERA etc.). Roche describes an extensive company‑sponsored Phase III programme and is preparing to present data and share with health authorities — consistent with active development and potential regulatory engagement.
drug_indication: Drug name: Giredestrant (also GDC-9545 / RO7197597 / RG6171). Specific indication covered by lidERA: Investigational adjuvant endocrine therapy for people with oestrogen receptor (ER)-positive, HER2-negative, early-stage (stage I–III) breast cancer at medium or high risk of recurrence.
mechanism_context: Giredestrant is an investigational, oral, next-generation selective oestrogen receptor degrader (SERD) and full antagonist. It is designed to block oestrogen from binding the oestrogen receptor, triggering receptor breakdown (degradation) and thereby stopping or slowing cancer cell growth. Targeting ER in ER-positive breast cancer aims to prevent oestrogen-driven tumour proliferation and overcome endocrine resistance; SERDs both antagonize and degrade ER protein, which can be advantageous versus therapies that only block signalling.
progress_timeline_date: Roche ad hoc announcement / media release date: 2025-11-18 — announced positive Phase III results from lidERA at a pre-planned interim analysis. ClinicalTrials.gov record: Last Update Posted 2025-11-21 (registry record updated shortly after the Roche announcement). Enrollment: Roche reported that over 4,100 patients were enrolled (no specific enrollment completion date stated in the press release). Interim analysis: Roche states the study met its primary endpoint at a pre-planned interim analysis; the press release date (2025-11-18) is the date of public announcement of that interim result. Additional milestone references: Roche notes lidERA is the second positive Phase III readout for giredestrant following evERA (results presented at ESMO Oct 17–21, 2025). Data from lidERA will be presented at an upcoming medical meeting and shared with health authorities. Missing / not found in scraped sources: The Roche release and the sections I accessed did not give explicit study start date, primary completion date or planned regulatory submission dates. The ClinicalTrials.gov record page is the best source for exact Study Start Date and Primary/Study Completion Dates; the registry record was last updated 2025-11-21. If exact start/primary completion dates are required, consult the ClinicalTrials.gov NCT04961996 record directly.
trial_design: Trial: lidERA Breast Cancer (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT04961996). Phase: Phase III, randomized, open-label, multicentre, global study. Arms: Experimental arm (Arm A): Giredestrant 30 mg orally once daily (Days 1–28 of each 28-day cycle) for up to 5 years or until disease recurrence or unacceptable toxicity. LHRH agonist is used for male and pre-/perimenopausal participants as appropriate. Active comparator (Arm B): Endocrine therapy of physician’s choice (standard-of-care endocrine monotherapy). Patient population: Participants with histologically confirmed ER-positive, HER2-negative early breast cancer (stage I–III) at medium or high risk of recurrence. An exploratory open-label substudy evaluates giredestrant + abemaciclib in a subset. Randomization & masking: Randomized, parallel assignment, open-label (no masking). Primary endpoint: Invasive disease-free survival (iDFS) excluding unrelated cancers in other organs (i.e., excluding second primary non-breast cancers). Key secondary endpoints: Overall survival (OS), iDFS including second primary non-breast cancers, disease-free survival, and safety. Enrollment: Roche reports over 4,100 patients enrolled in lidERA.
interim_analysis_results: At interim analysis, giredestrant significantly improved invasive disease‑free survival versus standard endocrine therapy; overall survival immature but positive trend; safety profile consistent with expectations and no unexpected findings.
regulatory_designations: FDA Fast Track Designation (Dec 15, 2020) for ER‑positive, HER2‑negative second/third‑line metastatic breast cancer
related_approved_in_trial_drugs: Tamoxifen, letrozole, anastrozole, exemestane, abemaciclib, olaparib
biomarker_requirements: ER‑positive and HER2‑negative status required; no other specific biomarker requirements noted.
geographic_scope: United States, multiple countries worldwide (~20 countries)
key_competing_drugs_trials: No key competing drugs/trials identified
current_standard_of_care: Adjuvant endocrine therapy (tamoxifen or AI) for 5–10 years; ovarian function suppression for premenopausal; 2 years abemaciclib plus endocrine therapy for high‑risk HR+/HER2‑ disease; 1 year olaparib for germline BRCA‑mutated high‑risk patients.
primary_endpoints: Invasive disease‑free survival (IDFS)
target_population: Medium‑ and high‑risk Stage I–III histologically confirmed ER‑positive, HER2‑negative early breast cancer; premenopausal/perimenopausal participants and males receive LHRH agonist as appropriate.
trial_progress_evidence: Study status Active, not recruiting; enrollment began 31‑Aug‑2021; 627 locations across ~20 countries; interim analysis met primary endpoint with significant iDFS benefit; OS trend positive.
previous_phase_results_summary: Prior clinical experience indicated giredestrant was well tolerated with encouraging antitumor activity as monotherapy and in combination with palbociclib in ER+ metastatic breast cancer; a positive Phase III readout in the advanced‑disease setting (evERA) preceded the lidERA interim analysis.
secondary_endpoints: Overall survival, disease‑free survival (DFS), distant recurrence‑free interval (DRFI), patient‑reported outcomes (EORTC QLQ‑C30 role functioning and global health status/quality of life)
comparator_control_arm: Physician’s choice adjuvant endocrine monotherapy limited to tamoxifen, letrozole, anastrozole, or exemestane; LHRH agonist for male and pre/perimenopausal participants per local practice.
study_design: Phase 3, randomized, parallel assignment, open-label, treatment intent; two‑arm comparison of adjuvant giredestrant monotherapy vs physician’s choice endocrine monotherapy; exploratory giredestrant + abemaciclib substudy.
safety_signals_modifications: No unexpected safety findings at interim analysis; no protocol modifications reported
mechanism_of_action: Giredestrant is an investigational oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) that antagonizes ER signaling, induces an inactive receptor conformation and promotes proteasome‑mediated degradation of the estrogen receptor.

title: sacituzumab tirumotecan (MK-2870)
description: sacituzumab tirumotecan (MK‑2870) is an investigational TROP2‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate being evaluated in a Phase 3 trial (NCT06074588) for non‑small cell lung cancer. The trial tests the drug alone or in combination with pembrolizumab (Keytruda) as a first‑line therapy for patients with PD‑L1‑positive disease. It is sponsored by Merck in collaboration with Sichuan Kelun‑Biotech and has received regulatory attention as a breakthrough candidate.
oncology_drug_check: Non‑small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) With EGFR Mutations (MeSH: Lung Neoplasms); Biological: Sacituzumab tirumotecan (ADC)
development_status: The sponsor is Merck Sharp & Dohme LLC, in collaboration with Kelun-Biotech for Greater China rights. Regulatory designation includes an FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation granted for sacituzumab tirumotecan in the relevant NSCLC population. Sponsor communications and the ClinicalTrials.gov record indicate active Phase 3 development. For up-to-date recruitment status (recruiting/active/suspended) and study record history for amendments, ClinicalTrials.gov should be consulted.
drug_indication: Public records for NCT06074588 indicate the trial is for previously-treated advanced or metastatic nonsquamous non-small cell lung cancer, with sponsor documents and trial listings specifying cohorts with EGFR mutations (e.g., exon 19del or exon 21 L858R) or other actionable genomic alterations. This differs from the user's initial description of a first-line PD-L1-positive study; the retrieved public records for NCT06074588 reference previously-treated disease and genomic-altered cohorts.
mechanism_context: Sacituzumab tirumotecan (sac-TMT; MK-2870) is an investigational antibody-drug conjugate that uses a TROP2-targeting monoclonal antibody (sacituzumab) to bind TROP2-expressing tumor cells. It carries a cytotoxic payload from the topoisomerase-1 inhibitor class linked via a novel, irreversible but hydrolyzable linker, with an average drug-to-antibody ratio of approximately 7.4. This TROP2 ADC strategy concentrates TOPO1-inhibitor cytotoxicity in epithelial tumors that overexpress TROP2.
progress_timeline_date: The ClinicalTrials.gov record for NCT06074588, sponsored by Merck Sharp & Dohme LLC, shows a 'Last Update Posted' date of 2025-11-12. Exact study start date and completion estimates were not present in the captured excerpts.
trial_design: The official title (from ClinicalTrials.gov) is "Sacituzumab Tirumotecan (MK-2870) Versus Chemotherapy in Previously Treated Advanced or Metastatic Nonsquamous Non-small Cell Lung Cancer" (Sponsor: Merck Sharp & Dohme LLC). The purpose (from MSD trial listing) is to evaluate sacituzumab tirumotecan versus chemotherapy (docetaxel or pemetrexed) in previously-treated NSCLC with EGFR mutations. Primary hypotheses include superiority for PFS (by RECIST 1.1 assessed by BICR) and overall survival (OS) versus chemotherapy in that cohort. Key endpoints called out in sponsor summaries are PFS per RECIST 1.1 (BICR) and OS as primary hypotheses. Detailed secondary endpoints, randomization details, masking, total planned enrollment, and dosing schedules were not fully included in the scraped excerpts and require consultation of the full protocol or complete ClinicalTrials.gov Study Plan section.
biomarker_requirements: Oncogenic driver alteration required (EGFR exon 19del/L858R or other specified alterations). PD‑L1 status is not required. TROP2 expression is a stratification factor, not an eligibility requirement.
geographic_scope: Germany, Spain, Czechia, Poland, Greece, France, Italy, Japan
key_competing_drugs_trials: Sacituzumab govitecan (EVOKE program), Datopotamab deruxtecan (Dato‑DXd) trials in NSCLC
current_standard_of_care: First‑line PD‑L1 ≥50%: pembrolizumab, cemiplimab, or atezolizumab monotherapy. PD‑L1 1‑49%: platinum doublet plus pembrolizumab or cemiplimab (e.g., carboplatin + pemetrexed + pembrolizumab for non‑squamous disease).
primary_endpoints: Progression‑free survival (PFS) per RECIST 1.1 by blinded independent central review in EGFR‑mutant NSCLC; Overall survival (OS) in EGFR‑mutant NSCLC
target_population: Adults (≥18 years) with advanced or metastatic non‑squamous NSCLC harboring EGFR exon 19del/L858R or other specified genomic alterations (ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E, NTRK fusions, MET exon 14 skipping, RET, less common EGFR mutations). Must have progressed after 1‑2 prior EGFR‑TKI lines (including third‑generation if T790M) and after one platinum‑based regimen. Measurable disease per RECIST 1.1, tumor tissue required, stable brain metastases allowed, active CNS disease excluded.
trial_progress_evidence: Recruiting; enrollment began November 2023 and is ongoing. Primary completion estimated May 2027; study completion estimated March 2030.
line_of_therapy: Previously treated (post‑TKI and post‑platinum); not first‑line.
previous_phase_results_summary: Phase 1/2 data showed ORR ~32‑34%, median PFS 8‑9.3 months, median OS ~13.9 months, with a manageable safety profile in EGFR‑TKI‑resistant NSCLC.
secondary_endpoints: PFS and OS in overall randomized population, objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), patient‑reported outcomes (EORTC QLQ‑C30, QLQ‑LC13), safety (adverse events, discontinuations).
comparator_control_arm: Investigator's choice chemotherapy (docetaxel 75 mg/m² Q3W or pemetrexed 500 mg/m² Q3W).
study_design: Global, randomized, open‑label, phase 3; 1:1 allocation of MK‑2870 versus investigator’s choice chemotherapy (docetaxel or pemetrexed).
safety_signals_modifications: No safety signals or protocol modifications reported
mechanism_of_action: Sacituzumab tirumotecan (MK‑2870) is a TROP2‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate composed of a humanized anti‑TROP2 antibody linked via a sulfonyl‑pyrimidine carbonate linker to the topoisomerase I inhibitor payload KL610023. The linker undergoes irreversible nucleophilic aromatic substitution, providing stability and delivering the cytotoxic payload intracellularly to induce DNA damage in TROP2‑expressing tumor cells.

title: Taletrectinib Phase 3 Adjuvant Trial in ROS1‑positive NSCLC (NCT07154706)
description: Phase 3, double‑blind, randomized trial evaluating taletrectinib as adjuvant therapy in ROS1‑fusion positive non‑small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) after complete surgical resection.
oncology_drug_check: Non‑small Cell Lung Cancer (MeSH D002289); drug: Taletrectinib
development_status: Taletrectinib is in active clinical development. It has prior clinical data from Phase I and regional/global Phase II trials demonstrating activity and tolerability in ROS1-positive NSCLC. An expanded access program (NCT06841874) also exists. The current Phase 3 adjuvant trial (NCT07154706) has an actual start date in August 2025, indicating its progression to registrational/confirmatory testing in the adjuvant setting. Nuvation Bio Inc. is listed as the sponsor for NCT07154706.
drug_indication: Taletrectinib (AB-106, DS-6051b) is indicated for the adjuvant treatment of ROS1-fusion positive stage IB-IIIA non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) following complete tumor resection.
mechanism_context: Taletrectinib (also referenced as AB-106 / DS-6051b) is an oral, selective type I tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting ROS1 and pan-NTRK (NTRK1/2/3). Preclinical enzymatic IC50 values reported are ROS1 0.207 nmol/L, NTRK1 0.622 nmol/L, NTRK2 2.28 nmol/L, and NTRK3 0.980 nmol/L, consistent with potent inhibition of ROS1 fusion-driven signaling and NTRK fusion signaling. ROS1 fusions are oncogenic drivers in a subset of NSCLC and other tumors; NTRK fusions similarly drive tumors in multiple histologies. Taletrectinib is positioned as a ROS1/NTRK TKI with activity against ROS1-driven disease and potential to address some resistance mutations.
progress_timeline_date: The study's actual start date is 2025-08-21. The estimated primary completion date is 2030-08-30, and the estimated study completion date is 2033-08-30. The estimated enrollment is 180 participants.
trial_design: This is a Phase 3, multicenter, interventional, randomized, parallel-assignment, triple-masked (participant, care provider, investigator) double-blind study comparing taletrectinib oral capsules (400 mg once daily) with a matching placebo. The patient population includes individuals with ROS1-fusion positive NSCLC who have undergone complete tumor resection, specifically stages IB, II, and IIIA. The primary endpoint is Disease-free survival (DFS) by investigator assessment, defined as the time from randomization to disease recurrence or death (from any cause in the absence of recurrence), with a time frame of up to approximately 5 years after the first patient is randomized. Selected secondary endpoints include DFS rates at 2, 3, 4, and 5 years, Overall Survival (OS) with follow-up up to approximately 7 years, and incidence of adverse events (AEs) up to approximately 5 years. The estimated enrollment is 180 participants.
regulatory_designations: Priority Review, Breakthrough Therapy Designation, Orphan Drug Designation
biomarker_requirements: Documented ROS1 rearrangement in the primary tumor by a validated local assay performed in CLIA‑certified or locally equivalent labs; adequate tissue for prospective central confirmatory testing; central confirmation required prior to randomization.
key_competing_drugs_trials: No key competing drugs/trials identified
current_standard_of_care: Current standard of care unknown
primary_endpoints: Disease‑free survival (DFS) by investigator assessment
target_population: Adults ≥18 years (or ≥20 per local regulations) with histologically confirmed stage IB, II, or IIIA NSCLC (AJCC 9th) who have undergone definitive curative surgery with negative margins, ECOG 0‑1, ROS1 fusion positive by validated assay, and adequate tissue for central testing.
trial_progress_evidence: Official Title confirms adjuvant ROS1+ early‑stage NSCLC; Study Start (Actual) 2025-08-21; Primary Completion (Estimated) 2030-08-30; Study Completion (Estimated) 2033-08-30; Enrollment (Estimated) 180; Sponsor listed as Nuvation Bio Inc.
previous_phase_results_summary: No previous phase results summary available
secondary_endpoints: DFS rates at 2, 3, 4, and 5 years, Overall Survival (OS), Incidence of adverse events (AEs)
comparator_control_arm: Placebo
study_design: Phase 3; Interventional; randomized; parallel assignment; placebo-controlled; double‑blind/triple‑masked
safety_signals_modifications: No safety signals or modifications reported
mechanism_of_action: Taletrectinib is an oral, CNS‑active, next‑generation ROS1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor with 11‑ to 20‑fold selectivity over TrkB.

title: Tarlatamab
description: Tarlatamab is a bispecific CD3×CD70 antibody under investigation in Phase 3 clinical trials for solid‑tumor indications. It is being evaluated as a combination therapy in highly anticipated oncology programs and is listed among other next‑generation bispecifics in the ASH 2025 preview of emerging assets.
oncology_drug_check: Extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC); DLL3-targeted bispecific T-cell engager (drug/biologic)
development_status: Tarlatamab (IMDELLTRA™) is under active development by Amgen, with multiple Phase 1b/2 and Phase 3 trials currently recruiting, ongoing, or planned. Safety signals from Phase I studies indicate that cytokine release syndrome (CRS) is common (~50% any grade), and grade ≥3 treatment-related adverse events occurred in approximately 30% of patients. Rarely, grade 5 pneumonitis was observed in one patient (1%) in the Phase I dataset.
drug_indication: Tarlatamab (IMDELLTRA™, AMG 757) is primarily indicated for small-cell lung cancer (SCLC), especially relapsed/refractory (3L+) and extensive-stage SCLC (ES-SCLC). It is also being studied in first-line and maintenance combinations for ES-SCLC.
mechanism_context: Tarlatamab is a bispecific T cell engager (BiTE) molecule designed to bind DLL3 on target cancer cells and CD3 on T cells, thereby driving T cell-dependent killing of tumor cells. Exploratory analysis suggests that selecting for increased DLL3 expression can result in increased clinical benefit.
progress_timeline_date: DeLLphi-201 (Phase I, NCT03319940) had a data cutoff of July 19, 2022, showing an ORR of 23.4%, median DOR of 12.3 months, and median OS of 13.2 months. DeLLphi-301 (Phase 2, NCT05060016) is listed as a phase 2 study in relapsed/refractory SCLC. Multiple Phase 3 trials (DeLLphi-304, -305, -306, -312) and larger randomized studies (NCT07005128) are planned or underway, with Amgen citing DeLLphi-304 (randomized phase 3 monotherapy vs SOC in second-line) and DeLLphi-305 (tarlatamab+durvalumab vs durvalumab maintenance). A Lancet Oncology combination study reported enrollment and data between August 31, 2022, and January 30, 2024. Exact Phase 3 start and readout dates are company-controlled, but multiple trials are active or starting between 2023–2025 with readouts anticipated across 2024–2026.
trial_design: DeLLphi-201 (NCT03319940) is a Phase I dose-escalation and expansion study with IV dosing, evaluating safety (DLTs), objective response (modified RECIST), DOR, PFS, and OS. DeLLphi-301 (NCT05060016) is an open-label, single-arm Phase II study in 3L+ relapsed SCLC, focusing on efficacy, safety, and PK. NCT05361395 is a Phase 1b study assessing the safety and tolerability of tarlatamab with PD-L1 inhibitors +/- chemotherapy in first-line settings. NCT04885998 involves combination trials and PK evaluations. NCT07005128 is a randomized study comparing tarlatamab+durvalumab+carboplatin/etoposide versus durvalumab+carboplatin/etoposide, with primary endpoints likely including PFS/OS.
interim_analysis_results: No interim analysis results available; primary analysis reported at ASCO 2025.
biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker requirements
key_competing_drugs_trials: Lurbinectedin, Topotecan, Platinum rechallenge, Amrubicin
current_standard_of_care: For second‑line relapsed SCLC, options include platinum rechallenge (if chemotherapy‑free interval >90 days) or single‑agent chemotherapy such as topotecan or lurbinectedin; amrubicin is used in some regions.
primary_endpoints: Overall survival (OS)
target_population: Adults with relapsed small cell lung cancer after platinum‑based first‑line chemotherapy, with disease progression after platinum with or without prior anti‑PD‑(L)1 therapy.
trial_progress_evidence: Primary analysis presented as a late‑breaking abstract at ASCO 2025 showing significant OS and PFS benefit; trial listed as active, not recruiting.
previous_phase_results_summary: Phase 1 (DeLLphi‑300) in heavily pretreated SCLC showed ORR 23.4%, median OS 13.2 months, with manageable safety; Phase 2 (DeLLphi‑301) showed a durable ORR that substantially exceeded Phase 1 with a more favorable safety profile, forming the basis for the Phase 3 trial.
secondary_endpoints: Progression‑free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DoR), patient‑reported outcomes (PROs), safety
comparator_control_arm: Physician’s choice standard‑of‑care chemotherapy (lurbinectedin, topotecan, or amrubicin per regional practice)
study_design: Randomized, open-label, global Phase 3 trial
safety_signals_modifications: Class‑consistent cytokine release syndrome (CRS) observed, primarily after the first two doses; managed with step‑up dosing; no protocol modifications reported.
mechanism_of_action: Bispecific T‑cell engager that binds DLL3 on tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, activating T cells and lysing DLL3‑expressing cells.

title: zocilurtatugpelitecan
description: zocilurtatugpelitecan is an investigational DLL3‑targeting antibody‑drug conjugate being evaluated in a Phase 3, controlled trial (NCT07218146) for patients with second‑line small‑cell lung cancer.
oncology_drug_check: Small Cell Lung Cancer; Antibody‑Drug Conjugate
development_status: Zocilurtatug pelitecan (ZL-1310) is in active development, currently undergoing a global Phase 3 clinical trial (NCT07218146).
drug_indication: Zocilurtatug pelitecan (ZL-1310) is indicated for the treatment of relapsed Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) in patients who have progressed on or after platinum-based first-line therapy as second-line therapy or after one line of chemotherapy followed by tarlatamab.
mechanism_context: Zocilurtatug pelitecan (ZL-1310) is a DLL3-targeted Antibody-Drug Conjugate (ADC).
progress_timeline_date: Zocilurtatug pelitecan (ZL-1310) is currently in a global Phase 3 clinical trial (NCT07218146). Updated Phase 1 data was announced on October 24, 2025, and the drug was highlighted at the AACR-NCI-EORTC 2025 conference on the same date. Specific start and end dates for the Phase 3 trial are not available in the provided information, but it is actively ongoing.
trial_design: The global Phase 3 trial (NCT07218146) for zocilurtatug pelitecan (ZL-1310) is designed as a randomized, open-label, parallel assignment study with a primary purpose of treatment. It aims to enroll approximately 665 patients across North America, Asia, and Europe. The patient population includes individuals with relapsed Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) who have progressed on or after platinum-based first-line therapy as second-line therapy or after one line of chemotherapy followed by tarlatamab. The study includes three arms: Arm 1 and Arm 2 involve ZL-1310 as a single agent at Dose Level 1 and Dose Level 2, respectively. Arm 3 serves as an active comparator, utilizing Investigator's Choice of Therapy, which may include Tarlatamab, Topotecan, Lurbinectedin, or Amrubicin. Primary endpoints are Overall Response Rate (ORR) and Overall Survival (OS), while secondary endpoints include Duration of Response (DOR), Progression-Free Survival (PFS), and safety.
biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker requirements
key_competing_drugs_trials: Tarlatamab-dlle, Lurbinectedin, Topotecan, Amrubicin, Rovalpituzumab tesirine, BI-1764532
current_standard_of_care: Relapsed SCLC is typically treated with lurbinectedin or topotecan; since May 2024 tarlatamab-dlle has received FDA accelerated approval and shows OS benefit versus chemotherapy.
primary_endpoints: Confirmed objective response rate (ORR) by BICR, Overall survival
target_population: Adults ≥18 with histologically/cytologically confirmed SCLC who have progressed after first-line platinum-based therapy; 2L tarlatamab allowed; measurable disease per RECIST v1.1; ECOG 0‑1; adequate organ function; brain metastases allowed under protocol; must provide tumor tissue/biopsy.
trial_progress_evidence: Status 'Not yet recruiting'; Study Start Date 2025-11-30; Primary Completion 2028-11-30; Estimated enrollment 665; company announcement confirms global enrollment and timeline.
previous_phase_results_summary: Phase 1a/1b study (NCT06179069) in 89 patients showed 2L SCLC ORR 67% overall and 79% at 1.6 mg/kg, disease control rate 97%, intracranial activity, and a favorable safety profile at doses <2.0 mg/kg.
secondary_endpoints: Duration of response (BICR and investigator), Progression‑free survival (BICR and investigator), Investigator‑assessed confirmed ORR, Time to response (BICR and investigator), Confirmed CNS response by BICR per RANO‑BM, Treatment‑emergent adverse events, Quality of life (EQ‑5D‑5L, EORTC‑QLQ‑C30, EORTC‑QLQ‑LC13)
comparator_control_arm: Investigator's Choice of Therapy: tarlatamab, topotecan, lurbinectedin, or amrubicin
study_design: Phase 3, interventional, randomized, parallel assignment, open-label, treatment
safety_signals_modifications: No safety signals or protocol modifications reported
mechanism_of_action: Humanized anti-DLL3 antibody-drug conjugate linked via a cleavable linker to a novel camptothecin derivative (topoisomerase I inhibitor) payload, built on the TMALIN platform.

title: patritumab deruxtecan (MK-1022)
description: Patritumab deruxtecan (MK-1022) is an investigational HER3‑targeted antibody‑drug conjugate being tested in a Phase 3, randomized, double‑blind, placebo‑controlled trial (NCT07060807) for HER3‑positive metastatic breast cancer. The study enrolls first‑line patients, uses progression‑free survival as the primary endpoint, and is sponsored by Merck in partnership with Daiichi Sankyo.
oncology_drug_check: Breast Neoplasms (MeSH: D001943); Intervention: Patritumab Deruxtecan (HER3‑DXd, MK‑1022), antibody‑drug conjugate
development_status: Patritumab deruxtecan is in an active global clinical development program, with the Phase 3 trial NCT07060807 registered and actively recruiting as of the last update on 2025-11-24. While the breast cancer program is ongoing, there was a voluntary withdrawal of a biologics license application (BLA) for patritumab deruxtecan in previously treated EGFR-mutated NSCLC on May 29, 2025. Safety monitoring for interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis is an important aspect of the development program, as these have been reported with DXd payload ADCs.
drug_indication: Patritumab deruxtecan (MK-1022; HER3-DXd; U3-1402) is being investigated for metastatic breast cancer (HER3-expressing), specifically in metastatic hormone-receptor-positive / HER2-negative breast cancer for the NCT07060807 trial. The global program aims to evaluate the drug across multiple HER3-expressing solid tumors.
mechanism_context: Patritumab deruxtecan (also called HER3-DXd, MK-1022, U3-1402) is an investigational HER3-targeted antibody-drug conjugate (ADC). The molecule consists of a fully human anti-HER3 IgG1 antibody linked to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (an exatecan derivative, “DXd”) via a tetrapeptide-based cleavable linker; ADC internalization and linker cleavage release the cytotoxic DXd payload into tumor cells.
progress_timeline_date: The ClinicalTrials.gov record for NCT07060807 was last updated on 2025-11-24. The registry includes various dates in 2025, such as July 2, 2025, and November 20, 2025, for fields like Start Date, Primary Completion Date, and Estimated Study Completion Date. The trial is actively recruiting internationally across numerous sites.
trial_design: The NCT07060807 trial is a Phase 3, interventional, randomized, parallel assignment study with an open-label masking. The experimental arm involves participants receiving patritumab deruxtecan via intravenous (IV) infusion every 3 weeks (Q3W) for approximately 13 months. The active comparator arm involves Treatment of Physician's Choice (TPC), which can include paclitaxel, nab-paclitaxel, capecitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, or trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) for up to 13 months. The primary endpoint is Progression-free survival (PFS). The key patient population includes patients with metastatic HER3-positive breast cancer, consistent with HR+/HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer in the HERTHENA program.
biomarker_requirements: HR+/HER2‑ disease status centrally confirmed; HER3 evaluable results from a metastatic or locally advanced lesion biopsy with central testing
key_competing_drugs_trials: Paclitaxel, nab‑paclitaxel, capecitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, trastuzumab deruxtecan, zenocutuzumab, seribantumab
current_standard_of_care: After progression on first‑line CDK4/6 inhibitor plus endocrine therapy, standard options include cytotoxic chemotherapy (e.g., taxanes, capecitabine), PARP inhibitors for germline BRCA/PALB2 mutations, trastuzumab deruxtecan for HER2‑low disease, and endocrine‑based approaches in endocrine‑sensitive patients.
primary_endpoints: Progression‑Free Survival (PFS) per RECIST 1.1 by blinded independent central review (BICR)
target_population: Adults with unresectable locally advanced or metastatic HR+/HER2‑ invasive breast carcinoma; centrally confirmed HR+ and HER2‑; HER3 evaluable biopsy required; must have progressed or recurred on prior CDK4/6 inhibitor plus endocrine therapy; other standard inclusion/exclusion criteria apply.
sponsor_developer: Merck (Merck Sharp & Dohme LLC) in partnership with Daiichi Sankyo
trial_progress_evidence: First posted 2025‑07‑11; last update 2025‑11‑24; last verified 2025‑11; recruiting sites in China, France, Hungary.
previous_phase_results_summary: In a multicohort phase 1/2 study (n=182) including 113 HR+/HER2‑ metastatic breast cancer patients, patritumab deruxtecan showed an ORR of 30.1% and median PFS of 7.4 months; grade ≥3 adverse events occurred in 71.4% of patients, with 9.9% discontinuations due to AEs and reported interstitial lung disease cases.
secondary_endpoints: Objective Response Rate (ORR) per RECIST 1.1 by BICR, Duration of Response (DOR), patient‑reported outcomes (EORTC QLQ‑C30 global health status, physical functioning), and additional efficacy/safety measures
comparator_control_arm: Active comparator Treatment of Physician’s Choice (paclitaxel, nab‑paclitaxel, capecitabine, liposomal doxorubicin, or trastuzumab deruxtecan)
study_design: Interventional, Phase 3; Randomized; Open-label; Parallel Assignment; Treatment purpose
safety_signals_modifications: No safety signals or protocol modifications reported
mechanism_of_action: Patritumab deruxtecan is a HER3‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate composed of a fully human IgG1 anti‑HER3 monoclonal antibody linked via a cleavable tetrapeptide linker to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (DXd, an exatecan derivative) with an approximate drug‑to‑antibody ratio of 8. Upon HER3 binding the ADC is internalized, the linker is cleaved in lysosomes, and the membrane‑permeable payload is released, causing DNA damage and a bystander antitumor effect.

title: Ifinatamab deruxtecan (MK-2400)
description: Ifinatamab deruxtecan (MK-2400) is a B7‑H3‑targeting antibody‑drug conjugate evaluated in a Phase 3 trial (NCT06644781) for patients with advanced esophageal cancer. The study assesses the drug as monotherapy in the first‑line setting, enrolling biomarker‑selected patients based on B7‑H3 expression.
oncology_drug_check: Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma (ESCC) – Antibody‑drug conjugate (ifinatamab deruxtecan)
development_status: Active/initiated: the program is active and the phase 3 trial has been initiated (first patient dosed May 19, 2025). The study is sponsored by Daiichi Sankyo and jointly developed with Merck. ClinicalTrials.gov record (NCT06644781) is maintained by Daiichi Sankyo and was updated in 2025.
drug_indication: Indication under study: unresectable advanced or metastatic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) in patients with disease progression following platinum‑based chemotherapy and an immune checkpoint inhibitor (i.e., second‑line setting).
mechanism_context: Ifinatamab deruxtecan (I-DXd, MK-2400) is an investigational antibody–drug conjugate (ADC) that targets B7‑H3. It is built on Daiichi Sankyo’s DXd platform: a humanized anti‑B7‑H3 IgG1 linked via tetrapeptide cleavable linkers to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (an exatecan derivative, DXd). The ADC is described as a potential first‑in‑class B7‑H3 directed DXd ADC.
progress_timeline_date: First patient dosed / trial initiation: May 19, 2025 (press release states “The first patient has been dosed in the IDeate‑Esophageal01 phase 3 trial…”). - ClinicalTrials.gov record: last update posted 2025‑09‑19 and lists the study as Phase 3 with estimated enrollment 510. - Estimated completion / primary completion (range): no explicit primary completion or final completion dates were available in the retrieved excerpts. Based on typical timelines for global randomized phase 3 OS trials enrolling ~500 patients in advanced solid tumors, a reasonable estimate for primary completion (to accumulate OS events) would be ~2–4 years after first patient in; i.e., a tentative range of 2027–2029 (estimate only; check ClinicalTrials.gov for official projected dates).
trial_design: Trial name: IDeate‑Esophageal01 (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT06644781). Key design features: - Phase: 3, interventional, randomized, global, multicenter, open‑label. - Arms/comparator: Ifinatamab deruxtecan 12 mg/kg IV versus investigator’s (physician’s) choice of chemotherapy (paclitaxel, docetaxel or irinotecan hydrochloride). - Patient population: patients with unresectable advanced/metastatic ESCC with disease progression after platinum‑based chemotherapy and an immune checkpoint inhibitor; eligible patients must have received no more than one prior line of systemic therapy in the advanced/metastatic setting. - Primary endpoint: overall survival (OS). - Key secondary endpoints: progression‑free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR) assessed by blinded independent central review, and safety. - Planned enrollment: approximately 510 patients across Asia, Europe and North America. - Dose listed in press release: 12 mg/kg. - Biomarker selection: trial materials and the press release emphasize B7‑H3 as the molecular target and note B7‑H3 overexpression in many cancers (including ESCC) but do not state that enrollment requires B7‑H3 positivity or other biomarker selection criteria in the excerpts retrieved. (In other words, the documented eligibility in the press release refers to prior therapies and disease state rather than explicit B7‑H3 expression thresholds.)
biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker requirements; B7‑H3 expression positivity is not required, though an adequate baseline tumor sample is needed and prior B7‑H3‑targeted agents are excluded.
geographic_scope: United States, China, France, Italy, Japan
key_competing_drugs_trials: Docetaxel, Paclitaxel, Irinotecan
current_standard_of_care: In the post‑platinum, post‑immune‑checkpoint‑inhibitor setting for advanced ESCC, single‑agent chemotherapies such as docetaxel, paclitaxel, or irinotecan are commonly used, which constitute the comparator arm of this trial.
primary_endpoints: Overall survival (OS)
target_population: Adults (≥18) with histologically or cytologically confirmed unresectable locally advanced or metastatic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC); disease progression after platinum‑based chemotherapy and an immune‑checkpoint inhibitor; at least one measurable lesion by RECIST v1.1; ECOG 0–1; adequate baseline tumor sample. Exclusions include prior B7‑H3‑targeted agents, prior topoisomerase I inhibitor, adenosquamous histology, inability to receive any comparator chemotherapy, certain local invasions, active CNS metastases, or significant corneal disease.
sponsor_developer: Daiichi Sankyo (responsible party) jointly developed with Merck (MSD)
trial_progress_evidence: Recruiting; first patient dosed and phase 3 initiation announced May 19, 2025; study start 2025‑03‑27; last update posted 2025‑09‑19.
previous_phase_results_summary: Early‑phase IDeate‑PanTumor01 reported an overall response rate of 32% (28% confirmed) in 118 patients with various solid tumors, including ESCC; a subgroup of pretreated small‑cell lung cancer showed an ORR of ~52.4%, supporting progression to this phase 3 trial.
secondary_endpoints: Progression‑free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DoR), disease control rate (DCR), safety
comparator_control_arm: Investigator's choice chemotherapy (docetaxel, paclitaxel, or irinotecan HCl)
study_design: Phase 3, interventional, randomized, open‑label, parallel comparison of I‑DXd monotherapy versus investigator's choice chemotherapy; primary purpose: treatment.
safety_signals_modifications: No safety signals or protocol modifications reported
mechanism_of_action: Ifinatamab deruxtecan (I‑DXd) is a B7‑H3‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate composed of an anti‑B7‑H3 IgG1 monoclonal antibody linked via a plasma‑stable, tumor‑selective cleavable tetrapeptide linker to a topoisomerase I inhibitor (DXd) payload, with an optimized drug‑to‑antibody ratio of ~4.

title: Ra223 and Enzalutamide Phase 3 Trial (NCT02194842)
description: The Phase 3 EORTC trial (NCT02194842) evaluates radium‑223 dichloride (Ra223) combined with enzalutamide in patients with asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) with bone‑only disease. The study randomizes 446 participants to Ra223 + enzalutamide versus enzalutamide alone. Primary endpoints are radiographic progression‑free survival (rPFS) by investigator assessment and centrally assessed progression‑free survival (PFS). Secondary endpoints include overall survival, time to first symptomatic skeletal event, pain assessments, and safety. An interim safety analysis showed reduced fracture rates with mandatory bone‑protecting agents. Enrollment began in October 2015, primary completion occurred in February 2024, and final study completion is projected for December 2028.
oncology_drug_check: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC); Drug
development_status: The trial has reported primary completion on 2024-02-19. Primary efficacy results have been published (Annals of Oncology, 2025), and EORTC communications indicate a confirmed overall survival benefit in later analyses. The trial is currently in active follow-up for final completion, estimated for December 2028. Enrollment is complete with 446 patients. Interim safety signals (fractures) were addressed by mandating bone-protective agents, which subsequently reduced fracture rates.
drug_indication: The trial drugs are Radium-223 dichloride (Ra-223) plus enzalutamide versus enzalutamide alone. The indication studied is asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) with bone-predominant (bone-only or bone-dominant) metastatic disease.
mechanism_context: Radium-223 (Ra-223; Xofigo) is an alpha-particle emitting radiopharmaceutical that preferentially localizes to areas of increased bone turnover (bone metastases) by forming complexes with bone mineral (hydroxyapatite). Its short-range, high-energy alpha emissions induce double-strand DNA breaks in adjacent tumor cells and bone marrow microenvironment, producing local cytotoxicity with limited systemic penetration. Enzalutamide (Xtandi) is a second-generation androgen receptor (AR) inhibitor that binds the AR and inhibits androgen binding, AR nuclear translocation, DNA binding and coactivator recruitment, thereby blocking AR-mediated transcription and tumor growth in prostate cancer.
progress_timeline_date: The study started in October 2015. Enrollment included 446 participants randomized 1:1, with the recruitment window from November 2015 to March 2023. Co-administration of bone-protecting agents was mandated as of March 2018. Primary completion was on 2024-02-19. The estimated study completion is December 2028. Key milestones include the publication of primary analysis (rPFS) in Annals of Oncology (2025) showing significant improvement with Ra-223, and subsequent overall survival analysis by EORTC confirming OS benefit. Interim safety analyses led to the bone-protecting agent mandate and showed reduced fracture rates when BPAs were used.
trial_design: This is a Phase 3, interventional, randomized, open-label, multicenter trial (EORTC-1333 / PEACE-3; ClinicalTrials.gov NCT02194842). The patient population consists of individuals with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), who are asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic (Brief Pain Inventory score <4), and have at least two bone metastases (bone-predominant disease). Patients were stratified by baseline pain, prior docetaxel, and bone-protecting agent use. The study arms are enzalutamide alone (160 mg daily) versus enzalutamide (160 mg daily) plus six cycles of Ra-223 (55 kBq/kg IV every 4 weeks). Randomization was 1:1. The primary endpoint is radiographic progression-free survival (rPFS) by investigator assessment. Key secondary endpoints include overall survival (OS), time to first symptomatic skeletal event (SSE), time to subsequent systemic treatment, pain progression, safety (adverse events, fractures), and quality of life measures. An interim safety analysis identified increased fractures without bone-protecting agents, leading to a mandate for co-administration of zoledronic acid or denosumab in 2018.
interim_analysis_results: Interim safety analysis identified a high fracture risk without bone‑protecting agents (49.2% fracture rate) and a reduced fracture rate when agents were mandated (23.2%). This led to a protocol amendment requiring bone‑protecting agents.
related_approved_in_trial_drugs: Enzalutamide, Abiraterone acetate, Radium-223 dichloride, Docetaxel, Cabazitaxel, Sipuleucel-T
biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker requirements
geographic_scope: Belgium, Brazil, Canada, Denmark, France, Ireland, Italy, Norway, Poland, Spain, Switzerland, United Kingdom
key_competing_drugs_trials: PREVAIL, ALSYMPCA, ERA-223
current_standard_of_care: Current standard of care unknown
primary_endpoints: Radiographic progression‑free survival (rPFS) by investigator assessment, Centrally assessed progression‑free survival (PFS)
target_population: Adults with metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) with bone‑only metastases, asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic (Brief Pain Inventory worst pain <4), no visceral disease, receiving ongoing ADT, performance status 0‑1, adequate organ function, required to start a bone‑protecting agent before radium‑223; prior docetaxel or abiraterone permitted only if given in the castration‑sensitive setting; no prior enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide or radium‑223.
sponsor_developer: European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer (EORTC); Collaborators: Bayer, Astellas Pharma Europe Ltd., UNICANCER, Canadian Urologic Oncology Group, Latin American Cooperative Oncology Group, Cancer Trials Ireland
trial_progress_evidence: Enrollment completed (446 participants); primary completion occurred on 2024‑02‑19; final study completion estimated for 2028‑12; interim safety analyses were performed and a protocol amendment mandated bone‑protecting agents.
previous_phase_results_summary: No previous phase results summary available
secondary_endpoints: Overall survival, Prostate cancer‑specific survival, Time to first symptomatic skeletal event, Time to first skeletal progression, Time to next systemic therapy, PFS2, Patient‑reported outcomes (pain, quality of life), Safety (adverse events including fractures)
comparator_control_arm: Enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily
study_design: Phase 3, randomized, open-label, parallel assignment, treatment purpose, 2 arms
safety_signals_modifications: A safety signal of increased fracture risk was identified; the protocol was modified to require bone‑protecting agents for all participants, which lowered the fracture incidence.
mechanism_of_action: Radium-223 is an alpha‑particle‑emitting calcium mimetic that localizes to bone metastases and induces high‑LET double‑strand DNA breaks in adjacent tumor cells; Enzalutamide is an androgen‑receptor signaling inhibitor that competitively blocks androgen binding, prevents nuclear translocation of the receptor, and inhibits AR‑DNA binding, leading to decreased prostate cancer cell growth and tumor regression.

title: Datroway (datopotamab deruxtecan) TROPION-Breast01
description: Datroway (datopotamab deruxtecan) TROPION‑Breast01 is a Phase III, open‑label study evaluating the TROP2‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate Datroway in patients with hormone‑receptor‑positive, HER2‑negative breast cancer who have received two or three prior lines of therapy.
oncology_drug_check: Breast Cancer (HR‑positive, HER2‑negative, and triple‑negative); Drug/Biologic (Antibody‑Drug Conjugate)
development_status: DATROWAY (datopotamab deruxtecan) has been approved in the U.S. for the treatment of adult patients with unresectable or metastatic HR-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer who have received prior endocrine-based therapy and chemotherapy. The TROPION-Breast01 trial completed its primary phase on 2024-07-24 and is estimated to complete entirely by 2025-12-31.
drug_indication: Datroway (datopotamab deruxtecan) is indicated for the treatment of adult patients with unresectable or metastatic hormone receptor (HR) positive, HER2 negative (IHC 0, IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-) breast cancer who have received prior endocrine-based therapy and chemotherapy for unresectable or metastatic disease.
mechanism_context: DATROWAY (datopotamab deruxtecan) is a TROP2-directed DXd antibody-drug conjugate (ADC). It is comprised of a humanized anti-TROP2 IgG1 monoclonal antibody attached to multiple topoisomerase I inhibitor payloads (an exatecan derivative, DXd) via tetrapeptide-based cleavable linkers.
progress_timeline_date: The TROPION-Breast01 trial started on 2021-10-18. Its primary completion date was 2024-07-24, and the estimated study completion date is 2025-12-31.
trial_design: TROPION-Breast01 is a global, randomized, multicenter, open-label Phase 3 study. It evaluates datopotamab deruxtecan versus investigator's choice of single-agent chemotherapy (eribulin, capecitabine, vinorelbine, or gemcitabine). The trial enrolled over 700 patients with inoperable or metastatic HR-positive, HER2-low or negative (IHC 0, IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-) breast cancer who have previously progressed on or are not suitable for endocrine therapy and have received at least one systemic therapy. The dual primary endpoints are progression-free survival (PFS) as assessed by blinded independent central review (BICR) and overall survival (OS). Key secondary endpoints include objective response rate (ORR), duration of response, investigator-assessed PFS, disease control rate, and time to first subsequent therapy.
interim_analysis_results: The trial demonstrated a statistically significant and clinically meaningful PFS benefit for Dato‑DXd versus investigator’s choice chemotherapy; OS follow‑up is ongoing. Results presented at major meetings showed improved efficacy and good tolerability.
estimated_enrollment: Approximately 700 patients; reported as “more than 700” enrolled
biomarker_requirements: HR‑positive and HER2‑low or HER2‑negative (IHC 0, IHC 1+, or IHC 2+/ISH). No TROP2 expression requirement reported.
geographic_scope: Asia, Europe, North America, South America, Africa
key_competing_drugs_trials: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (DESTINY‑Breast04), Sacituzumab govitecan (TROPiCS‑02)
current_standard_of_care: HR+/HER2‑negative metastatic breast cancer is typically treated first with endocrine therapy ± CDK4/6 inhibitor; upon progression, chemotherapy is used. Approved ADCs for this population include trastuzumab deruxtecan for HER2‑low disease and sacituzumab govitecan for HR+/HER2‑negative disease.
primary_endpoints: PFS by blinded independent central review (BICR), Overall survival (OS)
target_population: Adults with inoperable or metastatic HR‑positive, HER2‑low or negative breast cancer who have progressed on or are unsuitable for endocrine therapy and have received at least one prior systemic therapy (many had 1‑2 prior lines of chemotherapy).
sponsor_developer: AstraZeneca (sponsor) in collaboration with Daiichi Sankyo
trial_progress_evidence: First patient enrolled October 2021; all patients randomized; enrollment >700 across five continents; PFS primary endpoint met; positive press release issued; peer‑reviewed reports and congress presentations indicate efficacy and tolerability; OS follow‑up continues.
line_of_therapy: Post‑endocrine setting after ≥1 prior systemic therapy; protocol allowed 1‑2 prior lines of chemotherapy in the metastatic setting (2nd‑line+)
previous_phase_results_summary: No previous phase results summary available
secondary_endpoints: Objective response rate (ORR), Duration of response (DoR), Investigator‑assessed PFS, Disease control rate (DCR), Time to first subsequent therapy (TFST)
comparator_control_arm: Investigator’s choice of single‑agent chemotherapy (eribulin, capecitabine, vinorelbine, or gemcitabine)
study_design: Global, multicenter, randomized, open‑label, Phase 3
safety_signals_modifications: No protocol modifications reported publicly; safety described as well tolerated in comparative analyses.
mechanism_of_action: TROP2‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate (ADC) delivering a topoisomerase I inhibitor (DXd) payload.

title: ziftomenib Phase 3 trial in frontline AML
description: Ziftomenib is a menin inhibitor being evaluated in a global Phase 3, randomized, double‑blind, placebo‑controlled trial (NCT07007312) for newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukaemia (AML) patients with NPM1‑mutated or KMT2A‑rearranged disease. The study enrolls first‑line patients, combines ziftomenib with standard intensive or non‑intensive chemotherapy, and is sponsored by Kura Oncology, Inc. The trial began dosing in September 2025 and aims to demonstrate efficacy and safety versus placebo + chemotherapy.
oncology_drug_check: Frontline (untreated) NPM1‑mutated or KMT2A‑rearranged Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML); Intervention: ziftomenib — drug (small‑molecule menin inhibitor); MeSH: D015470; ICD‑10‑CM: C92.0
development_status: Ziftomenib is in active clinical development, with an ongoing Phase 3 program. ClinicalTrials.gov indicates the study is recruiting/ongoing, with the record last updated on 2025-10-22. Company press releases from 2025 describe the launch/first dosing and continued development collaborations. There is also ongoing clinical evaluation in earlier-line and relapsed/refractory settings.
drug_indication: The drug is Ziftomenib, an investigational oral menin inhibitor. It is being studied for newly diagnosed (first-line) acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with either NPM1 mutation (NPM1-m) or KMT2A rearrangement (KMT2A-r), given in combination with standard intensive or nonintensive chemotherapy backbones.
mechanism_context: Ziftomenib is an investigational oral menin inhibitor that disrupts the menin-dependent leukemogenic program (menin interaction with MLL/KMT2A), suppresses aberrant leukemogenic gene expression (eg, HOX/MEIS programs) and induces differentiation and anti-proliferative/apoptotic effects in NPM1-mutant and KMT2A-rearranged AML models. Preclinical and early clinical reports note differentiation induction and synergy with venetoclax. The biological rationale is that NPM1-mutant and KMT2A-rearranged AMLs are dependent on menin-mediated transcriptional programs; inhibiting menin is therefore expected to downregulate HOX/MEIS-driven leukemogenesis, providing a genotype-directed approach in these subtypes.
progress_timeline_date: The study record was last updated on 2025-10-22 according to ClinicalTrials.gov. Company disclosures indicate that trial dosing began in September 2025. Primary/Study completion is not specified as a fixed date on the record; however, the protocol states primary outcome assessment windows are up to 36 months after the last patient inclusion. Therefore, if enrollment began in September 2025, primary outcome assessments would extend into approximately 2028 (estimate) depending on the enrollment pace. Estimated enrollment was not provided in the extracted record.
trial_design: This is a Phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial with two parallel substudies: a Nonintensive Therapy Study and an Intensive Therapy Study. In the Nonintensive Therapy Study, patients receive venetoclax + azacitidine (SOC) + ziftomenib versus venetoclax + azacitidine + placebo. In the Intensive Therapy Study, patients receive induction with cytarabine + daunorubicin (7+3) + ziftomenib versus 7+3 + placebo; consolidation with cytarabine + ziftomenib/placebo; and maintenance with ziftomenib/placebo. The patient population includes adults with newly diagnosed, untreated AML harboring NPM1-mutation or KMT2A-rearrangement. The nonintensive cohort is for older or medically unfit patients, while the intensive cohort is for medically fit patients. The trial is double-blinded, meaning neither the patient nor the physician knows if the patient is receiving ziftomenib or placebo. Primary endpoints for the Nonintensive Therapy Study (all countries) include Overall Survival (OS), and for US & US reference countries, Complete Remission (CR) rate per ELN 2022 criteria. Primary endpoints for the Intensive Therapy Study (all countries) include Event-free Survival (EFS), and for US & US reference countries, CR rate per ELN 2022 criteria with central bone marrow MRD negativity in NPM1-m patients. All primary endpoints are assessed up to 36 months after the last patient inclusion. Secondary endpoints typically include safety, response duration, MRD, relapse-free survival, and other efficacy measures, with a full list available in the ClinicalTrials.gov Study Plan.
biomarker_requirements: NPM1-mutated required for the non‑intensive (VEN+AZA) cohort; NPM1‑mutated or KMT2A‑rearranged required for the intensive (7+3) cohort; intensive cohort also requires FLT3 wild‑type or ITD ratio <0.05 or ineligibility for FLT3‑targeted therapy; KMT2A partial tandem duplication is excluded.
key_competing_drugs_trials: Revumenib (SNDX-5613)
current_standard_of_care: Standard‑of‑care non‑intensive regimen is venetoclax plus azacitidine for patients unfit for intensive therapy; standard‑of‑care intensive regimen is cytarabine plus daunorubicin (7+3) for fit patients.
primary_endpoints: Overall survival (non‑intensive), CR rate per ELN 2022 (non‑intensive US), Event‑free survival (intensive), CR with bone marrow MRD negativity in NPM1‑mut patients (intensive US)
target_population: Adults ≥18 years with untreated AML per WHO 2022 criteria, ECOG 0‑2, adequate organ function. Non‑intensive cohort requires documented NPM1 mutation and ineligibility for intensive therapy (age ≥75 or age <75 with ECOG 2 or organ impairment). Intensive cohort requires documented NPM1 mutation or KMT2A rearrangement (excluding PTD), FLT3 wild‑type or ITD ratio <0.05 or ineligibility for FLT3‑targeted therapy, LVEF ≥50%, and fitness for intensive therapy. Key exclusions include prior AML therapy (except hydroxyurea/leukapheresis), APL, blast‑phase CML, BCR‑ABL mutation, active CNS leukemia, and uncontrolled leukocytosis.
trial_progress_evidence: Study start (actual) 2025‑09‑26; First posted 2025‑06‑05; Last update posted 2025‑10‑22; Recruiting at two U.S. sites (Yale and Wayne State).
previous_phase_results_summary: In the Phase 2 portion of KOMET‑001, ziftomenib 600 mg QD achieved a CR/CRh rate of 22% (95% CI 14–32), overall response rate of 33%, and 61% MRD negativity among responders. Early data from the KOMET‑007 combination study showed a 94% objective response rate in newly diagnosed NPM1‑mutated or KMT2A‑rearranged AML.
secondary_endpoints: Complete remission (EU & EU‑reference countries), bone marrow MRD negativity, CR+CRh, adverse events (NCI‑CTCAE v5.0), health‑related quality of life (EORTC QLQ‑C30)
comparator_control_arm: Placebo + venetoclax + azacitidine (non‑intensive study) and placebo + 7+3 induction + placebo cytarabine consolidation + placebo maintenance (intensive study).
study_design: Phase 3, randomized, double‑blind, placebo‑controlled, parallel assignment with two sub‑studies (non‑intensive VEN+AZA backbone and intensive 7+3 backbone).
safety_signals_modifications: No safety signals or modifications reported
mechanism_of_action: Ziftomenib is a potent, highly selective oral menin inhibitor that blocks the interaction of menin with MLL/KMT2A fusion proteins, thereby disrupting oncogenic transcription programs in AML.

title: IMDELLTRA® (tarlatamab-dlle)
description: IMDELLTRA® (tarlatamab-dlle) is a first‑in‑class DLL3‑targeting bispecific T‑cell engager approved by the FDA in November 2025 for adult patients with extensive‑stage small‑cell lung cancer (ES‑SCLC) whose disease has progressed after platinum‑based chemotherapy. Approval is based on the global Phase 3 DeLLphi‑304 trial, which showed a 40 % reduction in risk of death and extended median overall survival to 13.6 months versus 8.3 months with standard chemotherapy.
oncology_drug_check: extensive-stage small cell lung cancer; immunotherapy (bispecific T‑cell engager)
development_status: IMDELLTRA has received FDA full approval (November 2025). The development program remains active with multiple ongoing and planned DeLLphi studies (e.g., DeLLphi-303, -305, -306, -308, -309, -310, -311, -312) exploring combination therapies and different settings/dosing. Post-approval commercialization and ongoing clinical development are underway.
drug_indication: IMDELLTRA® (tarlatamab-dlle) is approved for adult patients with extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) whose disease has progressed during or after platinum-based chemotherapy. Approval was granted in November 2025 based on the DeLLphi-304 Phase 3 trial.
mechanism_context: IMDELLTRA (tarlatamab-dlle) is a first-in-class DLL3-targeting bispecific T-cell engager that redirects cytotoxic T cells to DLL3-expressing tumor cells, producing tumor cell killing via T-cell activation.
progress_timeline_date: DeLLphi-304 results were presented as a late-breaking presentation at the 2025 American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) Annual Meeting (June 2025) and simultaneously published in The New England Journal of Medicine (mid-2025; October 2025 correspondence). FDA full approval was granted in November 2025. The trial's enrollment and conduct are estimated to have spanned roughly 2022–2024/early-2025.
trial_design: The DeLLphi-304 trial is a randomized, global Phase 3 study. It compared tarlatamab monotherapy against physician’s choice standard-of-care chemotherapy (including topotecan, lurbinectedin, or amrubicin) in adult patients with extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) whose disease had progressed during or after platinum-based chemotherapy (second-line setting). The primary endpoint was Overall Survival (OS), with secondary endpoints including progression-free survival (PFS), patient-reported outcomes (PROs), and safety/tolerability. The primary analysis showed a substantial OS benefit with tarlatamab, reducing the risk of death by 40% and extending median OS to 13.6 months versus 8.3 months with standard chemotherapy. Improvements in PFS and favorable PROs and safety profile were also reported.
interim_analysis_results: Interim analysis demonstrated significantly improved overall survival (13.6 vs 8.3 months), progression‑free survival (HR 0.72), higher ORR (35% vs 20%), and lower rate of grade ≥3 adverse events (54% vs 80%) for tarlatamab versus chemotherapy.
regulatory_designations: Breakthrough Therapy, Priority Review, Orphan Designation (EMA)
biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker requirements
key_competing_drugs_trials: Topotecan, Lurbinectedin, Amrubicin
current_standard_of_care: Topotecan, Lurbinectedin, or Amrubicin (post‑platinum chemotherapy options).
primary_endpoints: Overall survival
target_population: Adults (≥18) with histologically/cytologically confirmed SCLC progressed/relapsed after one platinum‑based regimen; measurable disease per RECIST 1.1; ECOG 0‑1; adequate organ function; no symptomatic CNS metastases (exceptions allowed).
trial_progress_evidence: Status closed to accrual; prespecified interim analysis presented at ASCO 2025 and published; FDA granted full approval on Nov 19, 2025.
previous_phase_results_summary: Phase 2 DeLLphi‑301 showed antitumor activity with durable objective responses and promising survival outcomes in previously treated SCLC; safety signals included cytokine release syndrome and neurologic events.
secondary_endpoints: Progression‑free survival, patient‑reported outcomes (dyspnea), objective response rate
comparator_control_arm: Investigator's choice of topotecan, lurbinectedin, or amrubicin
study_design: Multicenter, randomized (1:1), open-label, phase 3
safety_signals_modifications: Boxed Warning for life‑threatening/fatal cytokine release syndrome and neurologic toxicity (ICANS); additional warnings for cytopenias, infections, hepatotoxicity, hypersensitivity, embryo‑fetal toxicity; step‑up dosing regimen (1 mg C1D1 then 10 mg on Days 8, 15 and q2w).
mechanism_of_action: Tarlatamab-dlle is a bispecific DLL3‑directed CD3 T‑cell engager that binds DLL3 on tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, redirecting T cells to lyse DLL3‑expressing cells.

title: ENHERTU (fam‑trastuzumab deruxtecan‑nxki)
description: ENHERTU (fam‑trastuzumab deruxtecan‑nxki) is being studied in a global, randomized, open‑label phase 3 trial (NCT04494425) in patients with hormone‑receptor‑positive, HER2‑low or HER2‑ultralow metastatic breast cancer who have received prior endocrine therapy. The trial compares ENHERTU monotherapy to investigator‑chosen chemotherapy, with PFS as the primary endpoint and OS, response rates and safety as secondary endpoints.
oncology_drug_check: Advanced or Metastatic Breast Cancer (MeSH: Breast Neoplasms, ICD-10: C50); Intervention: Drug (Trastuzumab deruxtecan)
development_status: The trial has reached primary endpoint analysis, with pivotal results reported and published in 2024. This indicates that the development of ENHERTU in this indication has advanced significantly. The sponsors are AstraZeneca and Daiichi Sankyo. ENHERTU already holds regulatory authorizations for HER2-directed indications, including a conditional marketing authorization in the EU from January 2021, and its labels cover HER2-low HR-positive metastatic breast cancer in multiple jurisdictions. For the current live recruitment status (e.g., Completed vs Active, not recruiting), the clinicaltrials.gov record (NCT04494425) should be consulted directly.
drug_indication: ENHERTU (fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki) is indicated for unresectable or metastatic breast cancer across multiple HER2-expression contexts (HER2-positive and HER2-low/ultralow HR-positive disease after prior therapies), and other tumor types with HER2 alterations per regulatory approvals. The clinical trial NCT04494425 specifically studies ENHERTU monotherapy in HR-positive, HER2-low or HER2-ultralow metastatic breast cancer after progression on endocrine therapy.
mechanism_context: ENHERTU (fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki; also called trastuzumab deruxtecan or T-DXd) is a HER2-directed antibody-drug conjugate (ADC). It combines a humanized anti-HER2 monoclonal antibody with a cleavable linker to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (a derivative of exatecan). This ADC has a high drug-to-antibody ratio and is designed to bind HER2 on tumor cells, be internalized, release the cytotoxic payload, and exert both intracellular cytotoxicity and a bystander killing effect in tumors with low HER2 expression. This mechanism is relevant to HER2-low and HER2-ultralow breast cancer because the ADC can act even when HER2 expression is not high (IHC 1+, IHC 2+/ISH-, and IHC 0 with membrane staining in ultralow), thereby expanding HER2-directed therapy to populations historically considered HER2-negative.
progress_timeline_date: The study start date, as listed on an institutional trial page, is 2021-07-05. Primary analyses and reporting occurred in 2024, with results presented and published in an ASCO/JCO abstract and a NEJM article. This indicates that primary completion and analysis were achieved prior to or during 2024. Given the publication of pivotal results in 2024, overall study completion (final analysis) is likely by mid-to-late 2024. Exact primary completion and study completion dates, as well as enrollment numbers, should be retrieved directly from the clinicaltrials.gov record (NCT04494425) if precise dates are required.
trial_design: This is a Phase 3, randomized, open-label, global, interventional trial comparing trastuzumab deruxtecan (ENHERTU) monotherapy with investigator's choice chemotherapy. The patient population includes adults with hormone receptor (HR)-positive, HER2-low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/ISH-) or HER2-ultralow (IHC 0 with membrane staining) advanced or metastatic breast cancer whose disease progressed on endocrine therapy. Specific prior therapy requirements include progression on at least 2 prior lines of endocrine therapies for metastatic disease or progression within 6 months of starting first-line endocrine therapy combined with a CDK4/6 inhibitor. The study arms consist of ENHERTU monotherapy versus investigator's choice chemotherapy (standard-of-care single-agent chemotherapies). The primary endpoint is progression-free survival (PFS), with key secondary endpoints including overall survival (OS), objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DoR), safety/tolerability, and patient-reported quality of life measures. The trial design also includes open-label and randomized allocation, with central testing for HER2 expression required.
biomarker_requirements: HR‑positive metastatic breast cancer with HER2‑low (IHC 2+/ISH‑ or IHC 1+) or HER2‑ultralow (IHC >0 <1+) by central testing in the metastatic setting; never previously HER2‑positive; adequate tumor sample.
geographic_scope: Asia, Europe, Australia, North America, South America
key_competing_drugs_trials: Disitamab vedotin (RC48) – NCT05904964, Trastuzumab duocarmazine (SYD985) – NCT03262935
current_standard_of_care: Investigator’s choice single‑agent chemotherapy such as capecitabine, paclitaxel, or nab‑paclitaxel after progression on endocrine therapy.
primary_endpoints: Progression‑free survival (PFS) by blinded independent central review in the HR‑positive, HER2‑low population
target_population: Adults (≥18 years) with pathologically documented advanced/metastatic HR‑positive breast cancer; HER2‑low (IHC 2+/ISH‑ or IHC 1+) or HER2‑ultralow (IHC 0 < IHC < 1+) by central testing on metastatic tissue; never previously HER2‑positive; no prior chemotherapy for advanced/metastatic disease; disease progression on endocrine therapy (e.g., within 6 months of first‑line ET + CDK4/6 inhibitor); adequate tumor sample.
trial_progress_evidence: Study started on 2020‑07‑24, primary completion on 2024‑03‑18, actual enrollment 866, global randomized phase 3 with specified arms.
line_of_therapy: Post‑endocrine therapy in the metastatic setting; no prior chemotherapy for advanced/metastatic disease allowed
previous_phase_results_summary: No previous phase results summary available
secondary_endpoints: PFS (BICR) in overall population (HER2‑low + HER2‑ultralow), overall survival (OS) in HER2‑low and overall populations, objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), time to first subsequent treatment or death, time to second subsequent treatment or death, safety
comparator_control_arm: Investigator’s choice single-agent chemotherapy (capecitabine, paclitaxel, or nab-paclitaxel)
study_design: Phase 3; randomized; multi-center; open-label; parallel assignment; sponsor-blind
safety_signals_modifications: No safety signals or modifications reported
mechanism_of_action: HER2‑directed antibody‑drug conjugate linking a trastuzumab‑based antibody to a membrane‑permeable topoisomerase I inhibitor (DXd) via a tumor‑selective cleavable linker; delivers DXd to HER2‑expressing cells with a by‑stander effect; drug‑to‑antibody ratio ~8.

title: Imdelltra
description: Imdelltra is a DLL3‑targeting T‑cell engager approved for second‑line treatment of extensive‑stage small cell lung cancer (SCLC). It demonstrated improved overall survival versus standard chemotherapy in the Phase 3 ASCENT‑07 trial (NCT05740566).
oncology_drug_check: Extensive-stage small cell lung cancer; drug/biologic
development_status: Following the positive Phase 3 DeLLphi-304 results, tarlatamab-dlle (IMDELLTRA) has received regulatory approval. The FDA granted traditional/full approval for tarlatamab-dlle for the treatment of adult patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) with disease progression on or after platinum-based chemotherapy, following a prior accelerated approval. The drug is now available for this labeled indication. The safety profile, as described in the prescribing information, includes treatment-emergent adverse events such as CRS, fatigue, decreased appetite, dysgeusia, pyrexia, constipation, musculoskeletal pain, nausea, and laboratory abnormalities like lymphopenia. The dose regimen in trials and the prescribing information specifies an initial 1 mg on Cycle 1 Day 1, followed by 10 mg on Days 8 and 15, and then every 2 weeks.
drug_indication: IMDELLTRA™ (tarlatamab-dlle) is indicated for the treatment of adult patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) with disease progression on or after platinum-based chemotherapy.
mechanism_context: Imdelltra (tarlatamab-dlle) is a DLL3-targeting bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) that binds delta-like ligand 3 (DLL3) on SCLC tumor cells and CD3 on T cells to redirect T-cell cytotoxicity to DLL3-expressing cancer cells.
progress_timeline_date: Amgen announced positive Phase 3 DeLLphi-304 results on June 2, 2025, reporting a statistically significant and clinically meaningful overall survival (OS) benefit. The company stated that IMDELLTRA significantly reduced the risk of death by 40% and extended median overall survival by more than five months compared to chemotherapy. The Phase 3 trial was estimated to have been planned and conducted approximately between 2022 and 2025, with the primary analysis and press release of results occurring in mid-2025.
trial_design: The DeLLphi-304 study (NCT05740566) is a Phase 3, randomized, open-label study.
